([/tʊər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*TOOR*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); French: [[tuʁ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Fr-Paris--Tours.ogg "File:Fr-Paris--Tours.ogg")) is the largest city in the region of [Centre-Val de Loire](/wiki/Centre-Val_de_Loire "Centre-Val de Loire"), France. It is the [prefecture](/wiki/Prefectures_in_France "Prefectures in France") of the [department](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France") of [Indre-et-Loire](/wiki/Indre-et-Loire "Indre-et-Loire"). The [commune](/wiki/Communes_of_France "Communes of France") of Tours had 138,668 inhabitants as of 2022 while the population of the whole [metropolitan area](/wiki/Functional_area_(France) "Functional area (France)") was 526,370.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

("hill of Caesar"). The name evolved in the 4th century when the original [Gallic](/wiki/Gaulish_language "Gaulish language") name, [Turones](/wiki/Turones "Turones"), became *Civitas Turonum* and then *Tours*. It was at this time that the [Tours Amphitheatre](/wiki/Tours_Amphitheatre "Tours Amphitheatre") was built.

**Tours** ([/tʊər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*TOOR*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); French: [[tuʁ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Fr-Paris--Tours.ogg "File:Fr-Paris--Tours.ogg")) is the largest city in the region of [Centre-Val de Loire](/wiki/Centre-Val_de_Loire "Centre-Val de Loire"), France. It is the [prefecture](/wiki/Prefectures_in_France "Prefectures in France") of the [department](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France") of [Indre-et-Loire](/wiki/Indre-et-Loire "Indre-et-Loire"). The [commune](/wiki/Communes_of_France "Communes of France") of Tours had 138,668 inhabitants as of 2022 while the population of the whole [metropolitan area](/wiki/Functional_area_(France) "Functional area (France)") was 526,370.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Tours sits on the lower reaches of the [Loire](/wiki/Loire "Loire"), between [Orléans](/wiki/Orl%C3%A9ans "Orléans") and the [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") coast. Formerly named Caesarodunum by its founder, Roman [Emperor Augustus](/wiki/Augustus "Augustus"), it possesses one of the largest amphitheaters of the Roman Empire, the [Tours Amphitheatre](/wiki/Tours_Amphitheatre "Tours Amphitheatre"). Known for the [Battle of Tours](/wiki/Battle_of_Tours "Battle of Tours") in 732 AD, it is a National Sanctuary with connections to the [Merovingians](/wiki/Merovingian_dynasty "Merovingian dynasty") and the [Carolingians](/wiki/Carolingian_dynasty "Carolingian dynasty"), with the [Capetians](/wiki/Capetian_dynasty "Capetian dynasty") making the kingdom's currency the [Livre tournois](/wiki/Livre_tournois "Livre tournois"). [Saint Martin](/wiki/Martin_of_Tours "Martin of Tours") and [Gregory of Tours](/wiki/Gregory_of_Tours "Gregory of Tours") were from Tours. Tours was once part of [Touraine](/wiki/Touraine "Touraine"), a former province of France. Tours was the first city of the silk industry. It was taken by [Louis XI](/wiki/Louis_XI_of_France "Louis XI of France"), as the royal capital under the [Valois Kings](/wiki/House_of_Valois "House of Valois") with its [Loire castles](/wiki/Loire_Valley "Loire Valley") and city of art with the School of Tours. The prefecture was partially destroyed during the [French Wars of Religion](/wiki/French_Wars_of_Religion "French Wars of Religion") in the late 16th century and again during the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") in June 1940.

The White and Blue city keeps a historical centre registered in the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO_Intangible_Cultural_Heritage_Lists "UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage Lists"), and is home to the Vieux-Tours, a [patrimonial](/wiki/Patrimonialism "Patrimonialism") site. The garden city has a green heritage and its urban landscape has been strongly influenced by its natural space. The historic city is nicknamed "*Le Petit Paris*". The city is a [university city](/wiki/University_of_Tours "University of Tours") with more than 30,000 students in 2019. Tours is a popular culinary city with specialties such as: [rillettes](/wiki/Rillettes "Rillettes"), rillons, [Touraine vineyards](/wiki/Loire_Valley_(wine) "Loire Valley (wine)"), AOC [Sainte-Maure-de-Touraine](/wiki/Sainte-Maure_de_Touraine "Sainte-Maure de Touraine") cheeses and [nougats](/wiki/Nougat "Nougat"). The city is also the end-point of the annual [Paris–Tours](/wiki/Paris%E2%80%93Tours "Paris–Tours") [cycle race](/wiki/Road_bicycle_racing "Road bicycle racing"). The region, due to its history and culture, has always been a land of birth or host to many personalities and international sporting events.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tours&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

A popular folk etymology of the word "Tours" is that it comes from *Turonus*, the nephew of [Brutus](/wiki/Brutus_of_Troy "Brutus of Troy"). Turonus died in a war between [Corineus](/wiki/Corineus "Corineus") and the king of [Aquitaine](/wiki/Aquitaine "Aquitaine"), [Goffarius Pictus](/wiki/Goffar_the_Pict "Goffar the Pict"), provoked by Corineus hunting in the king's forests without permission. It is said that Turonus was buried in Tours and the city is founded around his grave.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tours&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [Timeline of Tours](/wiki/Timeline_of_Tours "Timeline of Tours") and [Tours Amphitheatre in the ancient city](/wiki/Tours_Amphitheatre#In_the_ancient_city "Tours Amphitheatre")

In [Gallic](/wiki/Gaul "Gaul") times, Tours was an important crossing point over the [Loire](/wiki/Loire "Loire"). It became part of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") during the 1st century AD, and the city was named **Caesarodunum** ("hill of Caesar"). The name evolved in the 4th century when the original [Gallic](/wiki/Gaulish_language "Gaulish language") name, [Turones](/wiki/Turones "Turones"), became *Civitas Turonum* and then *Tours*. It was at this time that the [Tours Amphitheatre](/wiki/Tours_Amphitheatre "Tours Amphitheatre") was built.

Tours became a metropolis in the Roman province of [Lugdunum](/wiki/Lugdunum "Lugdunum") towards 380–388 AD, dominating [Maine](/wiki/Maine_(province) "Maine (province)"), Brittany, and the [Loire Valley](/wiki/Loire_Valley "Loire Valley"). One important figure in the city was Saint [Martin of Tours](/wiki/Martin_of_Tours "Martin of Tours"), a bishop who shared his coat with a naked beggar in [Amiens](/wiki/Amiens "Amiens"). The importance of Martin in the medieval Christian West made Tours, and its position on the route of pilgrimage to [Santiago de Compostela](/wiki/Santiago_de_Compostela "Santiago de Compostela"), a major centre during the Middle Ages.

### Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tours&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

In the 6th century, [Gregory of Tours](/wiki/Gregory_of_Tours "Gregory of Tours"), author of the *Ten Books of History*, restored a cathedral destroyed by a fire in 561. Saint Martin's monastery benefited from its inception, at the very start of the 6th century from patronage and support from the Frankish king, [Clovis I](/wiki/Clovis_I "Clovis I"), which increased considerably the influence of the saint, the abbey and the city in Gaul. In the 9th century, Tours was at the heart of the [Carolingian Renaissance](/wiki/Carolingian_Renaissance "Carolingian Renaissance"), in particular because of [Alcuin](/wiki/Alcuin "Alcuin"), of York in Northumbria, a renowned book collector and an abbot of [Marmoutier Abbey](/wiki/Marmoutier_Abbey_(Tours) "Marmoutier Abbey (Tours)").

In 732, [Abdul Rahman Al Ghafiqi](/wiki/Abdul_Rahman_Al_Ghafiqi "Abdul Rahman Al Ghafiqi") and an army of Muslim horsemen from [Al-Andalus](/wiki/Al-Andalus "Al-Andalus") advanced 500 kilometres (300 miles) deep into France, and were stopped at the [Battle of Tours](/wiki/Battle_of_Tours "Battle of Tours") at [Moussais-la-Bataille](/wiki/Vouneuil-sur-Vienne "Vouneuil-sur-Vienne")[[5]](#cite_note-5) (between [Châtellerault](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2tellerault "Châtellerault") and [Poitiers](/wiki/Poitiers "Poitiers")) by [Charles Martel](/wiki/Charles_Martel "Charles Martel") and his infantry. The Muslim army was defeated, preventing an Islamic conquest of France.

In 845, Tours repelled the first attack of the [Viking](/wiki/Viking "Viking") chief [Haesten](/wiki/Haesten "Haesten"). In 850, the Vikings settled at the mouths of the [Seine](/wiki/Seine "Seine") and the Loire. Still led by Haesten, they went up the Loire again in 852 and sacked [Angers](/wiki/Angers "Angers"), Tours and Marmoutier Abbey.

During the Middle Ages, Tours consisted of two juxtaposed and competing centres. The "City" in the east, successor of the late Roman '[castrum](/wiki/Gallo-Roman_enclosure_of_Tours "Gallo-Roman enclosure of Tours")', was composed of the cathedral and palace of the archbishops as well as the castle of Tours. The castle of Tours acted as a seat of the authority of the Counts of Tours (later Counts of Anjou) and the King of France. In the west, the "new city" structured around the Abbey of Saint Martin was freed from the control of the city during the 10th century (an enclosure was built towards 918) and became "Châteauneuf". This space, organized between Saint Martin and the Loire, became the economic centre of Tours. Between these two centres were Varennes, vineyards and fields, little occupied except for the Abbaye Saint-Julien established on the banks of the Loire. The two centres were linked during the 14th century.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Tudor_buildings_in_Tours%2C_France.jpg/250px-Tudor_buildings_in_Tours%2C_France.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tudor_buildings_in_Tours,_France.jpg)

*Place Plumereau*, Medieval buildings

Tours became the capital of the county of Tours or [Touraine](/wiki/Touraine "Touraine"), a territory bitterly disputed between [the counts of Blois](/wiki/Blois "Blois") and [Anjou](/wiki/County_of_Anjou "County of Anjou") – the latter were victorious in the 11th century. It was the capital of France at the time of Louis XI, who had settled in the castle of Montils (today the castle of Plessis-les-Tours, at the junction of the Loire and the Indre rivers). Tours and Touraine remained a permanent residence of the kings and court until the 16th century. The Renaissance gave Tours and Touraine many private mansions and castles, joined to some extent under the generic name of the Châteaux of the Loire. It is also at the time of Louis XI that the silk industry was introduced – despite difficulties, the industry still survives to this day.

### 16th–18th centuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tours&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 16th–18th centuries")]

Charles IX passed through the city at the time of his royal tour of France between 1564 and 1566, accompanied by the Court and various noblemen: his brother the [Duke of Anjou](/wiki/Duke_of_Anjou "Duke of Anjou"), [Henri de Navarre](/wiki/Henri_de_Navarre "Henri de Navarre"), the cardinals of Bourbon and [Lorraine](/wiki/Lorraine_(province) "Lorraine (province)"). At this time, the Catholics returned to power in Angers: the attendant assumed the right to nominate the aldermen. The [Massacre of Saint-Barthelemy](/wiki/Massacre_of_Saint-Barthelemy "Massacre of Saint-Barthelemy") was not repeated at Tours. The Protestants were imprisoned by the aldermen – a measure which prevented their extermination. The permanent return of the Court to Paris and then Versailles marked the beginning of a slow but permanent decline. Guillaume the Metayer (1763–1798), known as [Rochambeau](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_Donatien_de_Vimeur,_comte_de_Rochambeau "Jean-Baptiste Donatien de Vimeur, comte de Rochambeau"), the well-known counter-revolutionary chief of Mayenne, was shot in Tours.

### 19th to 20th centuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tours&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 19th to 20th centuries")]

The arrival of the railway in the 19th century saved the city by making it an important nodal point. The main railway station is known as Tours-Saint-Pierre-des-Corps. At that time, Tours was expanding towards the south into a district known as the Prébendes. The importance of the city as a centre of communications contributed to its revival and, as the 20th century progressed, Tours became a dynamic conurbation, economically oriented towards the service sector. The city was briefly the *de facto* capital of France during the [Franco-Prussian War](/wiki/Franco-Prussian_War "Franco-Prussian War") playing host to [Léon Gambetta](/wiki/L%C3%A9on_Gambetta "Léon Gambetta") and representatives of the [Government of National Defence](/wiki/Government_of_National_Defence "Government of National Defence") who escaped the then ongoing [Siege of Paris](/wiki/Siege_of_Paris_(1870-1871) "Siege of Paris (1870-1871)").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

#### First World War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tours&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: First World War")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Tours_Cathedral_Saint-Gatian.jpg/250px-Tours_Cathedral_Saint-Gatian.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tours_Cathedral_Saint-Gatian.jpg)

[Tours Cathedral](/wiki/Tours_Cathedral "Tours Cathedral"): 15th-century [Flamboyant](/wiki/Flamboyant "Flamboyant") Gothic west front with Renaissance pinnacles, completed 1547

The city was greatly affected by the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"). A force of 25,000 American soldiers arrived in 1917, setting up textile factories for the manufacture of uniforms, repair shops for military equipment, munitions dumps, an army post office and an American military hospital at Augustins. Because of this, Tours became a garrison town with a resident general staff. The American presence is remembered today by the [Wilson Bridge](/wiki/Wilson_Bridge_(Tours) "Wilson Bridge (Tours)") over the Loire, which was officially opened in July 1918 and bears the name of the President of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") from 1913 to 1921. Three American air force squadrons, including the 492nd, were based at the [Parçay-Meslay](/wiki/Par%C3%A7ay-Meslay "Parçay-Meslay") airfield, their personnel playing an active part in the life of the city. Americans paraded at funerals and award ceremonies for the Croix de Guerre. They also took part in festivals and their [YMCA](/wiki/YMCA "YMCA") organised shows for the troops. Some men married women from Tours.

#### Inter-war years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tours&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Inter-war years")]

In 1920, the city hosted the Congress of Tours, which saw the creation of the [French Communist Party](/wiki/French_Communist_Party "French Communist Party"). One future consequence of that congress was the presence of [Ho Chi Minh](/wiki/Ho_Chi_Minh "Ho Chi Minh"), the [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") nationalist, who became one of the first members of the party.

#### Second World War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tours&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Second World War")]

Tours was also marked by the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), as the city suffered massive destruction in 1940. For four years it was a city of military camps and fortifications. From 10 to 13 June 1940, Tours was the temporary seat of the French government before its move to [Bordeaux](/wiki/Bordeaux "Bordeaux").

German [incendiary bombs](/wiki/Incendiary_bombs "Incendiary bombs") caused a huge fire, which blazed out of control from 20 to 22 June and destroyed part of the city centre. Some architectural masterpieces of the 16th and 17th centuries were lost, as was the monumental entry to the city. The Wilson Bridge, which carried a water main that supplied the city, was dynamited to slow the progress of the German advance. With the water main severed, nobody could extinguish the inferno. That made inhabitants have no option but to flee to safety. More heavy air raids by Allied forces devastated the area around the railway station in 1944, causing several hundred deaths.

#### Post-war developments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tours&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Post-war developments")]

A plan for the rebuilding of the central area drawn up by the local architect [Camille Lefèvre](/wiki/Camille_Lef%C3%A8vre "Camille Lefèvre") had been adopted even before the end of the war. The plan was for 20 small quadrangular blocks of housing to be arranged around the main road (la [rue Nationale](/wiki/Rue_Nationale "Rue Nationale")), which was widened. This regular layout attempted to echo but simplify the 18th-century architecture. [Pierre Patout](/wiki/Pierre_Patout "Pierre Patout") succeeded Lefèvre as the architect in charge of rebuilding in 1945. At one time, there was talk of demolishing the southern side of the [rue Nationale](/wiki/Rue_Nationale "Rue Nationale") to make it in keeping with the new development.

The recent history of Tours is marked by the personality of [Jean Royer](/wiki/Jean_Royer "Jean Royer"), who was mayor for 36 years and helped save the old town from demolition by establishing one of the first [Conservation Areas](/wiki/Conservation_Areas "Conservation Areas"). This example of conservation policy would later inspire the Malraux Law for the safeguarding of historic city centres. In the 1970s, Jean Royer also extended the city to the south by diverting the course of the [Cher River](/wiki/Cher_(river) "Cher (river)") to create the districts of Rives du Cher and des Fontaines. At the time, it was one of the largest urban developments in Europe. In 1970, the [François Rabelais University](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Rabelais_University "François Rabelais University") was founded and centred on the bank of the Loire in the downtown area, not, as was the current practice, in a campus in the suburbs. The latter solution was also chosen by the twin university of Orleans. Royer's long term as mayor was, however, not without controversy, as is exemplified by the construction of the practical but aesthetically unattractive motorway, which runs along the bed of a former canal just 1,500 metres (4,900 feet) from the cathedral. Another bone of contention was the original Vinci Congress Centre by [Jean Nouvel](/wiki/Jean_Nouvel "Jean Nouvel"). The project incurred debts although it at least made Tours one of France's principal conference centres.

[Jean Germain](/wiki/Jean_Germain_(politician) "Jean Germain (politician)"), a member of the Socialist Party, became mayor in 1995 and made debt reduction his priority. Ten years later, his economic management was regarded as much wiser than that of his predecessor because of the financial stability of the city returning. However, his achievements were criticised by the municipal opposition for a lack of ambition. There were no large building projects instituted under his two terms. That position is disputed by those in power, who affirm their policy of concentrating on the quality of life, as evidenced by urban restoration, the development of public transport and cultural activities.

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tours&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Climate")]

Tours has an [oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") that is very mild for such a northern latitude. Summers are influenced by its inland position, resulting in frequent days of 25 °C (77 °F) or warmer, whereas winters are kept mild by Atlantic air masses. The entire valley between Orlans and Angers is famous for the luminosity of its air and for its fabulous châteaux, most of them Renaissance (over 600 between Orlans and Angers).

### Cathedral

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tours&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Cathedral")]

Main article: [Tours Cathedral](/wiki/Tours_Cathedral "Tours Cathedral")

The Cathedral of Tours, dedicated to [Saint Gatien](/wiki/Gatianus_of_Tours "Gatianus of Tours"), its [canonized](/wiki/Canonized "Canonized") first [bishop](/wiki/Bishop_of_Tours "Bishop of Tours"), was begun about 1170 to replace the cathedral that was burnt out in 1166 during the dispute between [Louis VII of France](/wiki/Louis_VII_of_France "Louis VII of France") and [Henry II of England](/wiki/Henry_II_of_England "Henry II of England"). The lowermost stages of the western towers belong to the 12th century, but the rest of the west end is in the profusely detailed 15th-century [Flamboyant Gothic](/wiki/Flamboyant_Gothic "Flamboyant Gothic"), which were completed just as the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") was affecting the patrons who planned the [châteaux](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teaux "Châteaux") of Touraine. The towers were being constructed at the same time as, for example, the [Château de Chenonceau](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Chenonceau "Château de Chenonceau").

When the 15th-century illuminator [Jean Fouquet](/wiki/Jean_Fouquet "Jean Fouquet") was set the task of illuminating [Josephus](/wiki/Josephus "Josephus")'s *Jewish Antiquities*, his depiction of [Solomon's Temple](/wiki/Solomon%27s_Temple "Solomon's Temple") was modelled on the nearly complete cathedral of Tours. The atmosphere of the Gothic cathedral close permeates [Honoré de Balzac](/wiki/Honor%C3%A9_de_Balzac "Honoré de Balzac")'s dark short novel of jealousy and provincial intrigues, *[Le Curé de Tours](/wiki/Le_Cur%C3%A9_de_Tours "Le Curé de Tours")* (*The Curate of Tours*) and his medieval story *[Maître Cornélius](/wiki/Ma%C3%AEtre_Corn%C3%A9lius "Maître Cornélius")* opens in the cathedral itself.

## Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tours&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Language")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Tours "Special:EditPage/Tours") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(May 2021)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Before the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), the inhabitants of Tours (*Les Tourangeaux*) were known for speaking the "purest" form of French in the entire country.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The pronunciation of Touraine was traditionally regarded as the most standard pronunciation of the French language, until the 19th century when the standard pronunciation of French shifted to that of the Parisian bourgeoisie.[[11]](#cite_note-11) That is explained by the fact that the court of France lived in [Touraine](/wiki/Touraine "Touraine") between 1430 and 1530. French, the language of the court, had become the official language of the entire kingdom.

A [Council of Tours in 813](/wiki/Third_Council_of_Tours "Third Council of Tours") decided that priests should preach sermons in different languages because the common people could no longer understand [classical Latin](/wiki/Classical_Latin "Classical Latin"). This was the first official recognition of an early French language distinct from Latin, and can be considered as the birth of French.

The Ordinance of [Montils-lès-Tours](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Plessis-lez-Tours "Château de Plessis-lez-Tours"), promulgated by [Charles VII](/wiki/Charles_VII_of_France "Charles VII of France") in 1454, made it mandatory to write laws and oral customs in the native language of the area.

An ordinance of [Charles VIII](/wiki/Charles_VIII_of_France "Charles VIII of France") (born in [Amboise](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_d%27Amboise "Château d'Amboise"), near Tours) in 1490 and one of [Louis XII](/wiki/Louis_XII_of_France "Louis XII of France") (born in [Blois](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Blois "Château de Blois"), near Tours) in 1510 broaden the scope of the ordinance of Charles VII.

Finally, the [Ordinance of Villers-Cotterêts](/wiki/Ordinance_of_Villers-Cotter%C3%AAts "Ordinance of Villers-Cotterêts"), signed into law by [Francis I](/wiki/Francis_I_of_France "Francis I of France") in 1539, called for the use of French in all legal acts, notarized contracts and official legislation to avoid any linguistic confusion.

[Gregory of Tours](/wiki/Gregory_of_Tours "Gregory of Tours") wrote in the 6th century that some people in the area could still speak [Gaulish](/wiki/Gaulish_language "Gaulish language").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Pont_Wilson_et_ligne_de_tramway_%C3%A0_Tours_en_soir%C3%A9e_%28juin_2023%29.JPG/250px-Pont_Wilson_et_ligne_de_tramway_%C3%A0_Tours_en_soir%C3%A9e_%28juin_2023%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Pont_Wilson_et_ligne_de_tramway_%C3%A0_Tours_en_soir%C3%A9e_(juin_2023).JPG)

Pont Wilson crosses the river [Loire](/wiki/Loire "Loire") at the old civic core.

The city has a population of 140,000 and is called "Le Jardin de la France" ("The Garden of France"). There are several parks located within the city. Tours is located between two rivers, the Loire to the north and the Cher to the south. The buildings of Tours are white with blue [slate](/wiki/Slate "Slate") (called *Ardoise*) roofs; this style is common in the north of France, while most buildings in the south of France have [terracotta](/wiki/Terracotta "Terracotta") roofs.

Tours is famous for its original medieval district, called *le Vieux Tours*. Unique to the Old City are its preserved [half-timbered](/wiki/Half-timbered "Half-timbered") buildings and *la Place Plumereau*, a square with busy pubs and restaurants, whose open-air tables fill the centre of the square. The Boulevard Beranger crosses the [Rue Nationale](/wiki/Rue_Nationale "Rue Nationale") at the Place Jean-Jaures and is the location of weekly markets and fairs.

Tours is famous for its many bridges crossing the river Loire. One of them, [Wilson Bridge](/wiki/Wilson_Bridge_(Tours) "Wilson Bridge (Tours)"), collapsed in 1978 but was rebuilt.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Tours_%2837%29_Jardins_de_l%27ancien_Archev%C3%AAch%C3%A9_-_C%C3%A8dre_du_Liban_-_02.jpg/250px-Tours_%2837%29_Jardins_de_l%27ancien_Archev%C3%AAch%C3%A9_-_C%C3%A8dre_du_Liban_-_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tours_(37)_Jardins_de_l%27ancien_Archev%C3%AAch%C3%A9_-_C%C3%A8dre_du_Liban_-_02.jpg)

Giant [cedar of Lebanon](/wiki/Cedrus_libani "Cedrus libani") tree

In the garden of the ancient Palais des Archevêques (now *Musée des Beaux-Arts*) is a huge [cedar](/wiki/Cedrus "Cedrus") tree said to have been planted by [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon").[[12]](#cite_note-12) The garden also has a stuffed elephant named Fritz. He escaped from the [Barnum and Bailey](/wiki/Barnum_and_Bailey "Barnum and Bailey") circus during their stay in Tours in 1902. He went mad and had to be shot down, but the city paid to honor him, and he was taxidermied as a result.

Tours is home to [University of Tours](/wiki/University_of_Tours "University of Tours") (formerly known as University François Rabelais of Tours), the site of one of the most important choral competitions, called *Florilège Vocal de Tours* International Choir Competition, and is a member city of the [European Grand Prix for Choral Singing](/wiki/European_Grand_Prix_for_Choral_Singing "European Grand Prix for Choral Singing").

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tours&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Tramway_de_Tours_11_%E2%80%93_gare_SNCF.JPG/250px-Tramway_de_Tours_11_%E2%80%93_gare_SNCF.JPG)](/wiki/File:Tramway_de_Tours_11_%E2%80%93_gare_SNCF.JPG)

Tramway of tours near [Tours railway station](/wiki/Tours_railway_station "Tours railway station"), designed by the French agency [RCP Design Global](/wiki/RCP_Design_Global "RCP Design Global")

Today, with extensive rail (including [TGV](/wiki/TGV "TGV")) and *[autoroute](/wiki/Autoroutes_of_France "Autoroutes of France")* connections linking to the rest of the country, Tours is a jumping-off point for tourist visits to the [Loire Valley](/wiki/Loire_Valley "Loire Valley") and the royal [châteaux](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau "Château").

Tours is on one of the main lines of the TGV. It is possible to travel to the west coast of [Bordeaux](/wiki/Bordeaux "Bordeaux") in an hour and forty minutes. From there, the line follows the [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean") coast via [Avignon](/wiki/Avignon "Avignon"), and then to Spain and [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona"). There are also lines to [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon "Lyon"), [Strasbourg](/wiki/Strasbourg "Strasbourg") and [Lille](/wiki/Lille "Lille"). It takes less than one hour by train to get from Tours to Paris by TGV and one and a half hours to get to [Charles de Gaulle Airport](/wiki/Charles_de_Gaulle_Airport "Charles de Gaulle Airport"). Tours has two main stations: [Gare de Tours](/wiki/Gare_de_Tours "Gare de Tours"), the central station, and [Gare de Saint-Pierre-des-Corps](/wiki/Gare_de_Saint-Pierre-des-Corps "Gare de Saint-Pierre-des-Corps"), used by trains that do not terminate in Tours.

[Tours Loire Valley Airport](/wiki/Tours_Loire_Valley_Airport "Tours Loire Valley Airport") connects the Loire Valley to European cities.

Historically, Tours was served by [trams](/wiki/Tram "Tram") and [trolleybuses](/wiki/Trolleybus "Trolleybus"), the [trolleybus system](/wiki/Trolleybuses_in_Tours "Trolleybuses in Tours") lasting from 1949 to 1968. Tram service returned to the city in 2013, when a new [tram system](/wiki/Tours_tramway "Tours tramway") began operation. Twenty-one [Alstom Citadis](/wiki/Alstom_Citadis "Alstom Citadis") trams were ordered.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

There is also a bus service, the main central stop being *Jean Jaurès*, next to the [Hôtel de Ville](/wiki/H%C3%B4tel_de_Ville,_Tours "Hôtel de Ville, Tours"), and *[rue Nationale](/wiki/Rue_Nationale "Rue Nationale")*, the [high street](/wiki/High_street "High street") of Tours. The tram and bus networks are operated by Fil Bleu and they share a ticketing system. A second tram line is scheduled for 2025.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tours&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Sport")]

The city's premier [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club, [Tours FC](/wiki/Tours_FC "Tours FC"), was dissolved in 2025, after 106 years of activity, due to years of financial struggles.[[17]](#cite_note-17) They also have a second team, [CCSP Tours](/wiki/CCSP_Tours "CCSP Tours"). CCSP's home stadium is the Stade des Tourettes and they play in the Division d'Honneur Regionale de Centre, the seventh tier of the [French football league system](/wiki/French_football_league_system "French football league system").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Tours has served as the finish location for [Paris–Tours](/wiki/Paris%E2%80%93Tours "Paris–Tours"), a one-day [road cycling](/wiki/Road_bicycle_racing "Road bicycle racing") [classic](/wiki/Classic_cycle_races "Classic cycle races") race held almost every October since 1896.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Tours also has a volleyball club named the [Tours VB](/wiki/Tours_VB "Tours VB").

## Catholics from Tours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tours&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Catholics from Tours")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/b/b7/LeoDupont.jpg)](/wiki/File:LeoDupont.jpg)

[Venerable Leo Dupont](/wiki/Leo_Dupont "Leo Dupont"), *Holy Man of Tours*

Tours is a special place for Catholics who follow the [devotion](/wiki/Catholic_devotions "Catholic devotions") to the [Holy Face of Jesus](/wiki/Holy_Face_of_Jesus "Holy Face of Jesus") and the adoration of the [Blessed Sacrament](/wiki/Blessed_Sacrament "Blessed Sacrament"). In 1843, Sister [Marie of St Peter](/wiki/Marie_of_St_Peter "Marie of St Peter") of Tours reported a vision which started the devotion to the [Holy Face of Jesus](/wiki/Holy_Face_of_Jesus "Holy Face of Jesus"), in reparation for the many insults Christ suffered in His Passion. The *[Golden Arrow Prayer](/wiki/The_Golden_Arrow_Holy_Face_Devotion_(Prayer) "The Golden Arrow Holy Face Devotion (Prayer)")* was first made public by her.

The [Venerable](/wiki/Venerable "Venerable") [Leo Dupont](/wiki/Leo_Dupont "Leo Dupont") also known as The [Holy Man of Tours](/wiki/Holy_Man_of_Tours "Holy Man of Tours") lived in Tours at about the same time. In 1849 he started the nightly adoration of the [Blessed Sacrament](/wiki/Blessed_Sacrament "Blessed Sacrament"), which spread throughout France. Upon hearing of Sister [Marie of St Peter](/wiki/Marie_of_St_Peter "Marie of St Peter")'s reported visions, he started to burn a vigil lamp continuously before a picture of the [Holy Face of Jesus](/wiki/Holy_Face_of_Jesus "Holy Face of Jesus"). The devotion was eventually approved by [Pope Pius XII](/wiki/Pope_Pius_XII "Pope Pius XII") in 1958 and he formally declared the Feast of the [Holy Face of Jesus](/wiki/Holy_Face_of_Jesus "Holy Face of Jesus") as Shrove Tuesday (the Tuesday before Ash Wednesday) for all Roman Catholics.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The [Oratory of the Holy Face](/wiki/Oratory_of_the_Holy_Face "Oratory of the Holy Face") on Rue St. Étienne in Tours receives many pilgrims every year.

Tours was the site of the episcopal activity of [St. Martin of Tours](/wiki/St._Martin_of_Tours "St. Martin of Tours") and has further Christian connotations in that the pivotal [Battle of Tours](/wiki/Battle_of_Tours "Battle of Tours") in 732 is often considered the first decisive victory over the invading Islamic forces, turning the tide against them. The battle also helped lay the foundations of the [Carolingian Empire](/wiki/Carolingian_Empire "Carolingian Empire").[[20]](#cite_note-20)
